The Water Cycle

evaporation → condensation → precipitation → collection

Earth's hydrological cycle is the continuous movement of water through the atmosphere, land, and oceans. Driven by solar energy and gravity, it circulates roughly 577,000 km³ of water every year — the same water that has existed on Earth for over 4 billion years.

577,000
km³ / year
Total Water Cycled
86%
of evaporation
From Oceans
505,000
km³ / year
Global Precipitation
10.6M
km³ stored
Freshwater Groundwater

Complete Cycle

Overview
graph TD
  OCEAN["Ocean & Surface Water"] -->|"solar heating"| EVAP["Evaporation"]
  PLANTS["Vegetation"] -->|"leaf release"| TRANS["Transpiration"]
  EVAP --> VAPOR["Water Vapor Rises"]
  TRANS --> VAPOR
  VAPOR -->|"cools at altitude"| COND["Condensation"]
  COND --> CLOUDS["Cloud Formation"]
  CLOUDS -->|"droplets grow heavy"| PRECIP["Precipitation"]
  PRECIP -->|"rain, snow, sleet, hail"| LAND["Land Surface"]
  PRECIP -->|"falls directly"| OCEAN
  LAND --> RUNOFF["Surface Runoff"]
  LAND --> INFILT["Infiltration"]
  RUNOFF -->|"rivers & streams"| OCEAN
  INFILT --> GW["Groundwater"]
  GW -->|"slow underground flow"| OCEAN
  GW -.->|"springs & wells"| LAND
  LAND -.->|"lakes & reservoirs"| EVAP

  classDef heat fill:#f9731622,stroke:#f97316,stroke-width:2px
  classDef atmo fill:#a78bfa22,stroke:#a78bfa,stroke-width:2px
  classDef precip fill:#22d3ee22,stroke:#22d3ee,stroke-width:2px
  classDef water fill:#3b82f622,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px
  classDef earth fill:#10b98122,stroke:#10b981,stroke-width:2px

  class EVAP,TRANS heat
  class VAPOR,COND,CLOUDS atmo
  class PRECIP,LAND precip
  class OCEAN,RUNOFF water
  class INFILT,GW,PLANTS earth
Evaporation / Heat
Atmosphere
Precipitation
Surface Water
Ground / Vegetation

Six Stages

Detailed
Stage 01 — Evaporation

Liquid → Vapor

Oceans, lakes, rivers, soil

Solar energy heats surface water, giving molecules enough kinetic energy to escape as vapor. Oceans account for 86% of all evaporation. Rate increases with temperature, wind speed, and low humidity.

H₂O(l) + energy → H₂O(g)
Stage 02 — Transpiration

Plants Release Water

Forests, grasslands, croplands

Plants absorb groundwater through roots and release water vapor through stomata in their leaves. A single oak tree can transpire over 150,000 litres per year.

Roots → Xylem → Stomata → Atmosphere
Stage 03 — Condensation

Vapor → Droplets

Atmosphere (1–12 km altitude)

Warm, moist air rises and cools. When it reaches the dew point, water vapor condenses around tiny particles (dust, pollen, sea salt) called condensation nuclei, forming clouds and fog.

H₂O(g) → H₂O(l) + heat released
Stage 04 — Precipitation

Water Falls to Earth

Atmosphere → Land & Ocean

Cloud droplets collide and merge until they are too heavy to stay suspended. Water returns to Earth as rain, snow, sleet, or hail. Globally, about 505,000 km³ falls annually.

Droplets coalesce → gravity wins
Stage 05 — Runoff & Collection

Water Flows Downhill

Rivers, streams, lakes, oceans

Precipitation that doesn't infiltrate the ground flows across the surface as runoff, collecting into streams and rivers that carry it back to lakes and oceans.

Gravity → streams → rivers → ocean
Stage 06 — Infiltration

Water Enters the Ground

Soil, rock, aquifers

Some precipitation soaks into the ground through permeable soil and rock, recharging aquifers. Groundwater moves slowly and can take centuries to reach the ocean.

Surface → soil pores → aquifer → discharge

Atmospheric Processes

Detail
graph LR
  SUN["Solar Radiation"] ==> HEAT["Surface Heating"]
  HEAT --> EV["Evaporation"]
  HEAT --> TR["Transpiration"]
  EV --> MV["Moist Air Mass"]
  TR --> MV
  MV -->|"rises and cools"| DEW["Dew Point Reached"]
  DEW --> NUC["Condensation Nuclei"]
  NUC --> DROPS["Cloud Droplets"]
  DROPS -->|"collision-coalescence"| BIG["Large Droplets"]
  DROPS -->|"ice crystal process"| ICE["Ice Crystals"]
  BIG --> RAIN["Rain"]
  ICE --> SNOW["Snow / Sleet / Hail"]
  RAIN --> FALL["Precipitation"]
  SNOW --> FALL

  classDef solar fill:#fbbf2422,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px
  classDef evapP fill:#f9731622,stroke:#f97316,stroke-width:2px
  classDef atmoP fill:#a78bfa22,stroke:#a78bfa,stroke-width:2px
  classDef precipP fill:#22d3ee22,stroke:#22d3ee,stroke-width:2px

  class SUN,HEAT solar
  class EV,TR,MV evapP
  class DEW,NUC,DROPS,BIG,ICE atmoP
  class RAIN,SNOW,FALL precipP

Surface & Subsurface Flow

Detail
graph TD
  PR["Precipitation"] --> SUR["Land Surface"]
  SUR --> RO["Surface Runoff"]
  SUR --> INF["Infiltration"]
  SUR --> DEP["Depression Storage"]
  DEP -.-> EV2["Evaporation"]
  RO --> ST["Streams"]
  ST --> RIV["Rivers"]
  RIV --> LAKE["Lakes"]
  RIV --> OCN["Ocean"]
  LAKE -.-> EV2
  INF --> SOIL["Soil Moisture Zone"]
  SOIL --> ROOTS["Root Uptake"]
  ROOTS --> TRANS2["Transpiration"]
  SOIL --> PERC["Percolation"]
  PERC --> AQ["Aquifer"]
  AQ -->|"base flow"| RIV
  AQ -->|"springs"| SUR
  AQ -->|"submarine discharge"| OCN

  classDef precipG fill:#22d3ee22,stroke:#22d3ee,stroke-width:2px
  classDef surfG fill:#3b82f622,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:1.5px
  classDef subG fill:#10b98122,stroke:#10b981,stroke-width:1.5px
  classDef evapG fill:#f9731622,stroke:#f97316,stroke-width:1.5px

  class PR precipG
  class SUR,RO,ST,RIV,LAKE,OCN,DEP surfG
  class INF,SOIL,PERC,AQ,ROOTS subG
  class EV2,TRANS2 evapG

Earth's Water Distribution

Data
ReservoirVolume (km³)% of TotalAvg. Residence TimePhase
OceansSaltwater, 97% of all water1,338,000,00096.5%~3,200 yearsLiquid
Ice Caps & GlaciersLargest freshwater store26,350,0001.74%~15,000 yearsSolid
GroundwaterFresh + saline aquifers23,400,0001.69%100–10,000 yearsLiquid
PermafrostFrozen soil moisture300,0000.022%~10,000 yearsSolid
LakesFresh + saline176,4000.013%~17 yearsLiquid
Soil MoistureRoot zone water16,5000.001%1–2 monthsLiquid
AtmosphereWater vapor + clouds12,9000.001%~9 daysVapor / Liquid
RiversActive flow channels2,1200.0002%~2 weeksLiquid
BiotaWater in living organisms1,1200.0001%~1 weekLiquid
Climate connection. The water cycle is tightly coupled to Earth's energy balance. Warmer temperatures accelerate evaporation, increasing atmospheric moisture by ~7% per 1°C of warming (Clausius–Clapeyron relation). This intensifies both droughts (faster drying) and heavy rainfall events — making the water cycle a key amplifier of climate change impacts.
